text.content: MINI PRD: P16 — Auto-Size Pipes in FluidFlow
Status: Plan
Batch: 2 | Priority: P16 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now

Focused how-to guide on FluidFlow's three pipe auto-sizing methods: Economic Velocity (default, using Generaux equation), By Velocity (user-specified target), and By Pressure Gradient (maximum allowable pressure drop per unit length). Includes step-by-step examples with screenshots showing pipe size selection, calculated velocities, and economic size recommendations. Strong procedural content with clear data entry instructions.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Maintain how-to clarity while adding decision guidance on WHICH sizing method to use for different applications. Add tables showing typical velocity ranges by fluid type and industry (e.g., liquid: 1-3 m/s general, 0.6-1.2 m/s for slurries, 15-30 m/s for gases). Include cost trade-off examples showing how pipe size affects capital cost, pressure drop, and pump power. Strengthen connection between auto-sizing and project economics.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that pipe sizing balances capital cost (larger pipes cost more) vs operating cost (smaller pipes need more pumping power) — FluidFlow's auto-sizing finds optimal diameters in seconds based on economic velocity, target velocity, or pressure constraints.

**Add "Sizing Method Selection Table"** (~200 words): Matrix showing when to use each method — Economic Velocity (general design, long-term operation), By Velocity (industry standards, code requirements, erosion limits), By Pressure Gradient (pressure-limited systems, long pipelines).

**Add "Typical Velocity Ranges by Fluid Type" table**: Liquids (1-3 m/s general, 0.6-1.2 m/s settling slurries, 1.5-2.5 m/s non-settling), Gases (15-30 m/s low pressure, 30-60 m/s high pressure), Two-Phase (varies by void fraction).

**Expand economic velocity explanation** (~250 words): Currently good but clarify that Generaux equation accounts for energy costs, pipe material costs, installation labor, and operating hours — updated regularly by Flite Software for current economics.

**Add "Cost Trade-off Example"** (~300 words): Show how 4" vs 5" vs 6" pipe affects total lifecycle cost (capital + 10-year energy cost). FluidFlow calculates exact economic size (e.g., 169.1mm) so engineers can evaluate nearest standard sizes.
**Add "Industry-Specific Guidance"**: Fire protection (typically Hazen-Williams, velocity limits per NFPA), Slurry (deposition velocity checks mandatory), Gas (choked flow risk at high velocities).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting auto-sizing speed to design iteration cycles and capital cost optimization.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).
**Fix any references to "radiation" heat transfer** — FluidFlow does convection/conduction only.
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Economic Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#economic-velocity) — in opening and Economic Velocity section
[Pressure Gradient](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#pressure-gradient) — in By Pressure Gradient section
[Deposition Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#deposition-velocity) — in slurry guidance
[Choked Flow](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#choked-flow) — in gas velocity limits
[Reynolds Number](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#reynolds-number) — in velocity validation
Internal Links
UP: P12 Equipment Sizing
SIDEWAYS: P11 Designer Handbook, P07 How to Build a Model, P21 Hydraulic Components - Pipes, P18 Defining a Slurry
BLOG: Link to "The Hidden Cost of Undersized Pipes" (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
0 — No selling message (procedural how-to content, let technical value speak)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pipe auto-sizing methods: Economic Velocity (Generaux equation), By Velocity, By Pressure Gradient
Pipe database: 14 standard pipe types with comprehensive size ranges
Economic velocity calculation: Based on fluid properties, energy costs, material costs, operating hours
Velocity validation: Warnings for high/low velocities outside typical ranges
Standards: ISO 5167 for orifice/nozzle sizing, NFPA guidance for fire protection
Fluid types: All 5 modules (Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase, Slurry, Non-Newtonian)
Heat transfer: Pipe heat loss/gain calculations affect density and therefore sizing
MINI PRD: P17 — Creating Fluid Streams
Status: Plan
Batch: 2 | Priority: P17 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1200-1800
What This Article Does Now

Procedural guide on defining fluid streams in FluidFlow from the comprehensive 1,283-fluid database. Covers simple Newtonian fluids, pure component fluids with temperature-dependent properties, custom fluid creation (5 types: Simple Newtonian, Pure Newtonian, non-Newtonian Liquid, Gas No Phase Change, Petroleum Fraction/Crude), and fluid mixing capabilities. Explains database navigation, property input methods, and when to use each fluid type.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Maintain clear procedural steps while adding decision guidance on WHICH fluid type to select based on system requirements. Add troubleshooting for common mistakes (using Fixed Properties when temperature varies significantly, selecting wrong EOS for gas type, missing phase change in two-phase systems). Include Quick Reference table mapping fluid categories to FluidFlow fluid types. Strengthen connection between proper fluid selection and calculation accuracy.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that accurate pipe network calculations require correct fluid property models — FluidFlow provides 1,283 database fluids plus custom fluid builders ensuring properties match actual process conditions, not textbook assumptions at 20°C.

**Add "Fluid Type Selection Matrix"** (~250 words): Decision tree showing when to use each of the 5 custom fluid types based on temperature range, phase behavior, rheology, and property data availability.

**Add "Database vs Custom Fluid" guidance** (~200 words): Explain when database fluids are sufficient (standard chemicals, common process fluids) vs when custom fluids are required (proprietary mixtures, non-standard conditions, petroleum fractions).

**Expand EOS selection guidance** (~300 words): Currently mentions Peng-Robinson, Lee-Kesler, BWRHS but needs context on WHICH to use WHEN. General guidance: Peng-Robinson for hydrocarbons/refrigerants, Lee-Kesler for non-polar gases, BWRHS for wide property ranges.

**Add "Temperature-Dependent Properties" section** (~250 words): Explain when temperature correlation data is critical (long pipelines with heat transfer, gas compression/expansion, two-phase systems) vs when fixed properties are acceptable (isothermal systems, short pipe runs, narrow temperature range).

**Add "Common Mistakes" callout**: Using Fixed Properties for gases (density changes with pressure), selecting Gas (No Phase Change) for systems that may flash, forgetting to enable heat transfer when temperature varies.
**Add "Validation Tip"**: After selecting fluid, check calculated properties (density, viscosity, vapor pressure) match expected values at system conditions.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper fluid selection to calculation accuracy and avoiding design errors.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Equation of State](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#equation-of-state) — in EOS selection section
[Vapor Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#vapor-pressure) — in two-phase considerations
[Viscosity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#viscosity) — in non-Newtonian section
[Density](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#density) — in property validation
[Phase Change](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#phase-change) — in fluid type selection
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P18 Defining a Slurry, P19 Non-Newtonian Viscosity Models, P26 Multi-Component Fluid Mixtures, P07 How to Build a Model
BLOG: Link to "Why Fluid Property Accuracy Matters" (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Free Training — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

5 — "1,283 fluids covering every process stream" — weave into opening: FluidFlow eliminates guessing with a comprehensive fluid database spanning water, gases, hydrocarbons, refrigerants, chemicals, and specialty fluids — plus custom builders for proprietary mixtures.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Fluid database: 1,283 fluids total
Custom fluid types: 5 options (Simple Newtonian, Pure Newtonian, non-Newtonian Liquid, Gas No Phase Change, Petroleum Fraction/Crude)
EOS options: Peng-Robinson, Lee-Kesler, BWRHS
Property models: Fixed properties, temperature correlation data, EOS-based calculations
Fluid mixing: Standard mixing rules for multi-component streams
Water properties: IFC-97 formulations (IAPWS)
Custom database: Users can add fluids permanently to database
Non-Newtonian rheology: 4 models (Power Law, Bingham Plastic, Casson, Herschel-Bulkley)
MINI PRD: P18 — Defining a Slurry in FluidFlow
Status: Plan
Batch: 2 | Priority: P18 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now

Focused guide on creating slurry fluid streams in FluidFlow, covering both settling slurries (solid particles in carrier fluid) and non-settling slurries (non-Newtonian homogeneous mixtures). Explains particle size distribution input, solids concentration (volumetric or mass-based), carrier fluid selection, and rheology model selection for non-Newtonian behavior. Includes pulp & paper stock flow setup with TAPPI correlation and Moller K data.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Maintain procedural clarity while adding critical engineering context on deposition velocity, settling vs non-settling classification, and correlation selection. Add decision tree for choosing between 8 settling slurry correlations based on particle size, concentration, and pipe orientation. Include validation checklist (velocity above deposition limit, Reynolds number appropriate for correlation, particle size within method range). Strengthen connection to real-world slurry applications (mining, dredging, wastewater).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that slurry flow systems require careful velocity selection to prevent settling and pipe blockage — FluidFlow calculates deposition velocity limits and excess pressure drop using 8 validated correlations for settling slurries plus 4 rheology models for non-Newtonian flows.

**Add "Settling vs Non-Settling Classification" section** (~250 words): Explain that particles below ~0.4mm typically stay suspended (non-settling, treat as non-Newtonian), while coarser particles settle (require deposition velocity checks). FluidFlow handles both.

**Add "Correlation Selection Guide" table**: Matrix showing which of the 8 settling correlations to use based on particle size range, concentration, and pipe inclination. Reference Wilson-Addie-Sellgren-Clift as primary reference.

**Expand deposition velocity section** (~400 words): Explain that deposition velocity is the minimum velocity to prevent particle settling. FluidFlow calculates multiple deposition criteria (WASC, GIW VSCALC, particle size function) plus characteristic velocities (Oroskar-Turian, Schiller-Herbich). Engineers must verify design velocity exceeds all limits.

**Add "Vertical Piping Considerations"** (~250 words): Settling behavior differs in vertical pipes — FluidFlow provides 3 vertical methods (Vertical Pipe WASC Loss, 4CM, Spelay-Gillies-Hashemi-Sanders 2017 Collisional Stress Model).
**Add "Pulp & Paper Setup" subsection** (~200 words): Expand on TAPPI correlation and Moller K data requirements. FluidFlow includes 16 pulp type options for TAPPI method.

**Add "Common Mistakes" callout**: Operating below deposition velocity (pipe blockage risk), extrapolating correlations beyond validated particle size ranges, using settling correlations for fine slurries (should use non-Newtonian).
**Add "Validation Checklist"**: Velocity > deposition limit, solids concentration within correlation range, particle size distribution matches correlation assumptions.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper slurry modeling to avoiding pipe blockage and optimizing pump power.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Deposition Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#deposition-velocity) — in opening and deposition section
[Slurry](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#slurry) — in opening
[Carrier Fluid](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#carrier-fluid) — in settling classification section
[Solids Concentration](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#solids-concentration) — in input data section
[Rheology](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#rheology) — in non-Newtonian section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P17 Creating Fluid Streams, P19 Non-Newtonian Viscosity Models, P16 Auto-Size Pipes, P11 Designer Handbook
BLOG: Link to "Slurry Pipeline Design: Avoid These 5 Costly Mistakes" (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

6 — "Works across every flow regime" — weave into opening: FluidFlow handles settling slurries, non-settling slurries, pulp & paper stock, and non-Newtonian fluids from a single interface, eliminating the need for separate software tools.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Slurry Module capabilities: Settling and non-settling slurries, pulp & paper stock
Settling correlations: 8 methods (Vsm, V50, 4CM, Durand, Wasp, WASC, Sellgren-Wilson, Liu Dezhong)
Non-Newtonian friction: Darby, Chilton-Stainsby, Converted Power Law
Rheology models: Power Law, Bingham Plastic, Casson, Herschel-Bulkley
Deposition velocity: 3 methods (WASC, particle size function, GIW VSCALC)
Characteristic velocities: Oroskar-Turian, Schiller-Herbich, Wilson-GIW, Thomas 1979/2015, Wilson 1992
Vertical piping: 3 methods (Vertical Pipe WASC, 4CM, Spelay et al. 2017)
Pulp & paper: TAPPI correlation, Moller K correlation, 16 pulp type options
Solids database: 11+ common materials (metals, coal, sands)
Pump derating: 5 methods for slurry pump performance adjustment
MINI PRD: P19 — Non-Newtonian Viscosity Models
Status: Plan
Batch: 2 | Priority: P19 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-2500
What This Article Does Now

Comprehensive guide to non-Newtonian fluid modeling in FluidFlow, covering the 4 rheology models (Power Law, Bingham Plastic, Casson, Herschel-Bulkley) and 3 friction loss calculation methods (Darby, Chilton-Stainsby, Converted Power Law). Explains shear-thinning vs shear-thickening behavior, yield stress concepts, and how to fit rheology curves to laboratory data. Includes R² coefficient for curve-fit accuracy and default database for common non-Newtonian liquids.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Elevate from model documentation to strategic non-Newtonian design guide. Add engineering context explaining WHEN each rheology model is appropriate (Power Law for polymer solutions, Bingham for drilling muds, Casson for blood/chocolate, Herschel-Bulkley for complex slurries). Include practical guidance on obtaining rheology data from lab tests, interpreting shear rate ranges, and validating curve fits. Strengthen connection between proper rheology selection and accurate pressure drop prediction for paints, food products, pharmaceuticals, and mining applications.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that non-Newtonian fluids (paints, polymers, slurries, food products) exhibit viscosity that changes with shear rate — accurate pipe network design requires selecting the correct rheology model and FluidFlow provides 4 validated models matching laboratory rheometer data.

**Add "Rheology Model Selection Guide" table** (~400 words): Matrix showing which model to use for which fluid types. Power Law (no yield stress, simple polymers), Bingham Plastic (constant yield stress, drilling muds/toothpaste), Casson (gradual yield, chocolate/blood/printing inks), Herschel-Bulkley (general model, complex slurries).

**Expand "Obtaining Rheology Data" section** (~350 words): Explain that rheology models require lab testing with rotational viscometer or rheometer. Engineers need shear stress vs shear rate data across expected pipe flow range. FluidFlow fits curves to this data with R² validation.

**Add "Shear Rate Range Considerations"** (~300 words): Pipe flow typically operates at 10-1000 s⁻¹ shear rates. Lab data must cover this range or extrapolation introduces errors. FluidFlow warns if operating outside validated shear rate range.

**Add "Friction Loss Method Selection"** (~300 words): Explain differences between Darby, Chilton-Stainsby, and Converted Power Law methods. Darby is general-purpose, Chilton-Stainsby for time-dependent fluids, Converted Power Law for simple Power Law fluids.

**Add "Common Fluids by Rheology Type" examples**: Power Law (CMC solutions, xanthan gum), Bingham (bentonite muds, cement slurries), Casson (molten chocolate, blood plasma), Herschel-Bulkley (wastewater sludge, mineral slurries).
**Add "Temperature Effects" mention**: Non-Newtonian viscosity often strongly temperature-dependent. FluidFlow handles temperature-dependent rheology parameters if data available.
**Add "Validation Approach"**: Compare calculated pressure drop to pilot plant data or published correlations for known fluids to validate rheology model selection.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper non-Newtonian modeling to avoiding undersized pipes and pump failures.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Viscosity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#viscosity) — in opening
[Shear Rate](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#shear-rate) — in rheology model section
[Yield Stress](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#yield-stress) — in Bingham/Herschel-Bulkley sections
[Reynolds Number](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#reynolds-number) — in friction loss section
[Rheology](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#rheology) — in opening
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P18 Defining a Slurry, P17 Creating Fluid Streams, P13 Quality Assurance & Standards, P11 Designer Handbook
BLOG: Link to "Non-Newtonian Fluids: Why Generic Viscosity Values Fail" (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact (for rheology testing guidance)
Selling Message

6 — "Works across every flow regime" — weave into opening: FluidFlow handles Newtonian, non-Newtonian, settling slurries, and two-phase flows from a single interface, eliminating the need for separate calculation tools for each fluid type.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Rheology models: 4 types (Power Law, Bingham Plastic, Casson, Herschel-Bulkley)
Friction loss methods: Darby, Chilton-Stainsby, Converted Power Law
Curve fitting: R² coefficient for accuracy validation
Default database: Common non-Newtonian liquids included
Custom fluid creation: non-Newtonian Liquid type with user-defined rheology parameters
Temperature dependence: Viscosity variation with temperature if data available
Slurry Module: Integrated with settling slurry calculations for complex systems
Yield stress: Can be calculated or user-specified
Shear rate validation: Warnings for operation outside validated ranges
MINI PRD: P20 — FluidFlow Troubleshooting Guide
Status: Plan
Batch: 2 | Priority: P20 | Type: Troubleshooting | Word Target: 2500-3500
What This Article Does Now

Comprehensive troubleshooting reference for resolving common FluidFlow warnings and building stable models. Covers 118 built-in alerts organized by severity (errors, warnings, information). Addresses convergence failures, high velocities, NPSH margin violations, choked flow conditions, cavitation warnings, and invalid boundary conditions. Includes systematic debugging approach and message interpretation guidance. Strong problem-solving structure with diagnostic steps.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Maintain troubleshooting structure while adding MORE specific solutions for the most common 15-20 issues. Reorganize by problem type (won't calculate, calculates but warns, unexpected results) rather than alphabetical message listing. Add "Quick Fixes" callouts for frequent issues (forgot to set boundary pressure, pipe too small for flow rate, pump curve doesn't intersect system curve). Include "When to Contact Support" guidance for complex convergence failures. Strengthen practical problem-solving focus.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that FluidFlow's 118 built-in alerts guide engineers toward stable, accurate models — this troubleshooting guide provides solutions for the 20 most common warnings, organized by problem type for faster resolution.

**Reorganize by problem category** (~1500 words total): Group issues as (1) Model Won't Calculate (convergence failures, missing boundaries, disconnected nodes), (2) High Velocity/Pressure Warnings (pipe undersized, choked flow, excessive ΔP), (3) NPSH/Cavitation Warnings (pump suction issues, vapor pressure exceeded), (4) Unexpected Results (flow direction reversed, negative pressures, unrealistic values).

**Add "Top 10 Quick Fixes" table**: Most common issues with one-line solutions (e.g., "High velocity warning → increase pipe diameter or check flow rate input", "NPSH violation → lower fluid temperature or raise tank elevation").

**Expand convergence failure section** (~400 words): Explain that convergence failures typically mean conflicting boundary conditions (two pressure boundaries with no flow path, pump curve doesn't reach required head). Provide systematic diagnostic steps.

**Add "Choked Flow" subsection** (~300 words): Explain that gas systems can reach sonic velocity limits. FluidFlow auto-detects choked flow but warns if downstream boundary conditions are incompatible. Solution: increase pipe size or reduce flow rate.
**Add "Negative Pressure" subsection** (~250 words): Explain causes (siphon effect, pump suction below vapor pressure, incorrect boundary elevations) and solutions.

**Add "When Results Look Wrong" section** (~300 words): Validation checklist — do flow directions match expectations, are velocities reasonable for fluid type, does total pressure drop match hand calculations (±20%), are pump duty points within curve range.
**Add "When to Contact Support"**: Complex convergence failures, suspected software bugs, results that don't match known benchmark cases, custom correlation requests.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting troubleshooting speed to project timelines and learning curve reduction.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Convergence](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#convergence) — in convergence failure section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in pump troubleshooting section
[Cavitation](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#cavitation) — in negative pressure section
[Choked Flow](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#choked-flow) — in gas system warnings
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in pump convergence issues
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P11 Designer Handbook, P07 How to Build a Model, P14 Modeling Centrifugal Pumps, P16 Auto-Size Pipes
BLOG: Link to "5 Model-Building Mistakes Every Engineer Makes Once" (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact (for technical support)
Selling Message

8 — "Engineers supporting engineers" — weave into opening and support section: FluidFlow's 118 alerts were developed over 40 years of customer feedback, and when standard troubleshooting doesn't resolve issues, our engineer-staffed support team provides expert guidance within hours, not days.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Alert system: 118 built-in warnings/errors/information messages
Convergence diagnostics: Automatic detection of conflicting boundary conditions
Velocity warnings: High/low velocity alerts with recommended ranges by fluid type
NPSH calculations: Automatic NPSHa calculation with NPSHr comparison and warnings
Choked flow detection: Auto-detection of sonic velocity limits in gas systems
Cavitation warnings: Vapor pressure comparison with local static pressure
Pressure warnings: Negative pressure detection, excessive pressure drop alerts
Flow direction validation: Ensures physically realistic flow patterns
Boundary validation: Checks for missing/incompatible boundary conditions
Support: Technical support provided by engineers (40+ years collective experience)
MINI PRD: P21 — Hydraulic Components - Pipes
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P21 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 2000-2500
What This Article Does Now

Component glossary page describing pipe elements in FluidFlow, covering standard pipes, rectangular/annulus ducts, and flexible/corrugated hoses. Documents the 14 pipe material types in the database (Steel, Stainless Steel, PVC, Copper, etc.) with size ranges and schedule/classification options. Explains pipe properties (diameter, length, roughness, elevation) and how FluidFlow calculates friction losses. Includes heat transfer options and pipe scaling for degradation modeling.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Transform from component reference to strategic pipe selection guide. Add engineering context explaining HOW material selection affects friction loss, WHEN to use rectangular ducts vs circular pipes, and WHY pipe roughness matters for accurate pressure drop. Include material comparison table showing typical roughness values and corrosion resistance by fluid type. Strengthen connection between proper pipe specification and system accuracy. Add cross-references to calculation methods and auto-sizing features.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that pipes are the primary pressure-loss elements in any network — FluidFlow models 14 standard pipe materials with complete size databases, rectangular/annulus geometries, and heat transfer integration, ensuring accurate system predictions from first design iteration.

**Add "Pipe Material Selection Guide" table** (~400 words): Matrix showing material suitability by fluid type and temperature. Steel (general purpose, -20 to 400°C), Stainless Steel (corrosive fluids, high purity), PVC (water/chemicals, <60°C), Copper (HVAC, potable water), etc.

**Expand "Pipe Roughness" section** (~350 words): Explain that roughness (ε) directly affects friction factor in Moody correlation. New pipes vs aged pipes can differ significantly (new steel ~0.045mm, aged ~0.15mm). FluidFlow's pipe scaling feature models degradation over time.

**Add "Circular vs Rectangular Ducts" comparison** (~300 words): Explain when rectangular/annulus ducts are appropriate (HVAC systems, space-constrained installations) and how FluidFlow converts to equivalent diameter for friction calculations.

**Add "Heat Transfer Options" subsection** (~300 words): Expand on the 5 heat transfer modes (buried pipe, pipe heat loss/gain, fixed heat rate, fixed temperature change, ignore). Explain that temperature changes affect density and therefore pressure drop, especially in gas systems.

**Add "Pipe Scaling for Degradation"** (~250 words): Explain that pipe fouling, corrosion, and scale buildup increase roughness over time. FluidFlow's scaling feature lets engineers model end-of-life performance during initial design.
**Add "Standard vs Custom Pipe Sizes"**: Database includes standard schedules (10, 40, 80, 160) but users can add custom sizes for proprietary equipment.
**Add "Validation Tip"**: After pipe selection, verify Reynolds number is appropriate for correlation (turbulent for most industrial systems, check laminar warnings).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting accurate pipe modeling to avoiding undersized systems and pump over-specification.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Pipe Roughness](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#pipe-roughness) — in roughness section
[Reynolds Number](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#reynolds-number) — in validation section
[Friction Factor](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#friction-factor) — in pressure drop section
[Equivalent Diameter](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#equivalent-diameter) — in rectangular duct section
[Darcy-Weisbach](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#darcy-weisbach) — in calculation method reference
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P16 Auto-Size Pipes, P11 Designer Handbook, P13 Quality Assurance & Standards, P07 How to Build a Model
BLOG: Link to "How Pipe Roughness Affects Your System Design" (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
0 — No selling message (technical component reference, let accuracy speak)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pipe database: 14 standard materials (Steel, Stainless Steel, ABS, Aluminum, Asbestos Cement, Cast Iron, Concrete, Copper, Ductile Iron, Flexible Hose, Glass, PE, PVC, PP/PFA)
Pipe types: Standard circular, rectangular/annulus, flexible/corrugated hose
Schedules: 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160, STD, XS, XXS (varies by material)
Pipe scaling: Models roughness increase due to fouling/corrosion/age
Heat transfer: 5 modes (buried pipe, heat loss/gain, fixed heat rate, fixed ΔT, ignore)
Pressure drop: Moody (Darcy-Weisbach), Hazen-Williams, Shell-MIT, Fixed Friction Factor
Auto-sizing: Economic velocity, By Velocity, By Pressure Gradient
Custom sizes: Users can add non-standard sizes to database
MINI PRD: P22 — Multi-Calc Feature Overview
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P22 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now

Guide to FluidFlow's Multi-Calc feature (part of Scripting Module) that automates parametric studies by varying one or more input parameters across defined ranges. Explains how to set up multi-variable calculations, define parameter ranges (linear, logarithmic, custom lists), and export results tables for comparison. Useful for sensitivity analysis, optimization studies, and design alternative evaluation. Includes examples of varying pipe diameter, flow rate, or fluid temperature to observe system response.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Elevate from feature documentation to strategic design optimization guide. Add engineering context explaining WHEN parametric studies matter (pipe diameter optimization, pump speed selection, seasonal flow variations), HOW to interpret multi-calc results (identifying optimal points, constraint violations), and WHY automated variation beats manual iteration. Include real-world scenarios: evaluating 4" vs 5" vs 6" pipe across 10 flow rates, VFD speed optimization across operating range, temperature effects on NPSH margin. Strengthen business case for parametric analysis.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that optimal pipe network design often requires evaluating dozens of parameter combinations (pipe sizes, flow rates, pump speeds) — FluidFlow's Multi-Calc automates this iteration, running hundreds of scenarios in minutes vs days of manual recalculation.

**Add "When to Use Multi-Calc" section** (~300 words): Design optimization (find most economic pipe size), sensitivity analysis (how does NPSH margin vary with temperature), constraint verification (ensure all flow scenarios meet velocity limits), operational planning (summer vs winter performance).

**Add "Multi-Calc vs Manual Iteration" comparison** (~250 words): Manual: change parameter, recalculate, record results, repeat 50 times. Multi-Calc: define range once, FluidFlow runs all cases automatically, exports table of results. Time savings: hours to minutes.

**Expand "Parameter Types" section** (~300 words): Explain that ANY model input can be varied — pipe diameter, flow rate, boundary pressure, fluid temperature, pump speed, valve position. Multiple parameters can be varied simultaneously (e.g., pipe size AND flow rate for matrix analysis).

**Add "Results Interpretation" section** (~400 words): Explain how to identify optimal solutions from multi-calc output tables. Example: Find pipe size where velocity stays between 1-3 m/s across all flow scenarios. Look for constraint violations (NPSH margin negative, velocity excessive, pressure below minimum).

**Add "Real-World Examples" (~400 words): (1) Pipe diameter optimization: Vary size from 4" to 10", identify minimum size meeting velocity/pressure constraints. (2) VFD optimization: Vary pump speed from 1200 to 1800 RPM, find speed for target flow rate with minimum power. (3) Seasonal analysis: Vary temperature from 5°C to 40°C, verify NPSH margin always positive.

What This Article Does Now

Focused how-to guide on FluidFlow's pump auto-sizing capability that selects pumps based on desired flow rate or pressure rise. Explains how to enable auto-sizing mode, specify target flow or head, and have FluidFlow recommend pump selections from the database or generate custom pump curves. Includes step-by-step procedure with data entry fields and results interpretation. Covers both centrifugal and positive displacement pumps.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Maintain procedural clarity while adding strategic context on WHEN to use auto-sizing vs manufacturer curves, HOW to validate recommended pump selections, and WHY starting with auto-sizing accelerates early design. Add decision guidance on flow-based vs pressure-based sizing modes. Include validation checklist (duty point within curve range, NPSH adequate, efficiency acceptable, power consumption reasonable). Strengthen connection between auto-sizing and preliminary design speed.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that early-stage design often requires pump sizing before specific equipment is selected — FluidFlow's auto-size generates appropriate pump curves based on system requirements, letting engineers finalize pipe layouts and confirm feasibility before vendor selection.

**Add "Auto-Size vs Manufacturer Curves" section** (~300 words): Explain workflow — use auto-size for preliminary design and system layout, then replace with manufacturer curves for detailed design and procurement. Auto-size ensures system is pumpable before committing to vendor quotes.

**Add "Flow-Based vs Pressure-Based Sizing" comparison** (~250 words): Flow-based: Specify target flow rate, FluidFlow calculates required head from system curve. Pressure-based: Specify target pressure rise, FluidFlow calculates resulting flow rate. When to use each mode.

**Expand validation section** (~350 words): After auto-sizing, engineers must verify: (1) NPSH margin adequate (NPSHa > NPSHr by 0.5+ meters), (2) Pump efficiency reasonable (typically 60-85% for centrifugal), (3) Power consumption matches available motors, (4) Duty point not at extreme ends of curve.

**Add "Integration with Pump Database"** (~250 words): FluidFlow can auto-size based on database pump curves (selecting best match from vendors) or generate custom theoretical curves. Database approach preferred when matching standard product lines.
**Add "Positive Displacement Pumps" subsection** (~200 words): Auto-sizing for PD pumps differs from centrifugal — flow rate is independent of head (within limits). FluidFlow handles both types.

**Add "Multiple Pump Scenarios"** (~200 words): Auto-size can explore parallel pumps (2 x 50% vs 1 x 100% capacity) or series pumps (two stages for high head). Reference P24 for detailed parallel/series modeling.

**Add "Transition to Vendor Selection"**: Once auto-sized system is validated, export pump specifications (flow, head, NPSH, power) for RFQ to vendors. Replace auto-size with actual manufacturer curves for final design.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting auto-sizing to faster feasibility studies and avoiding over/under-specified pumps.

MINI PRD: P24 — Modeling Pumps in Parallel & Series
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P24 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-2500
What This Article Does Now

Comprehensive guide to modeling parallel and series pump configurations in FluidFlow. Explains how parallel pumps increase flow capacity (at same head) while series pumps increase head capacity (at same flow). Covers combined pump curve generation, system curve interaction, duty point calculation for each configuration, and when each arrangement is appropriate. Includes practical examples showing how FluidFlow calculates flow distribution among parallel pumps and total head from series pumps.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Strengthen engineering context explaining WHY parallel vs series configurations are selected, HOW to avoid common mistakes (unequal parallel pump curves, inadequate NPSH in series), and WHEN to use duty/standby vs simultaneous operation. Add decision framework for parallel vs series selection based on system requirements. Include troubleshooting guidance for parallel pump imbalance and series pump cavitation. Strengthen connection to real-world applications (cooling towers, booster stations, high-rise buildings).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that complex piping systems often require multiple pumps for reliability, capacity, or head requirements — FluidFlow models parallel pumps (increased flow) and series pumps (increased head) with accurate flow distribution and combined curve generation, ensuring proper pump selection from initial design.

**Add "Parallel vs Series Decision Matrix"** (~350 words): Table showing when to use each configuration. Parallel: High flow demand, redundancy/reliability, duty/standby operation, cooling water systems. Series: High head requirements, multistage booster stations, high-rise buildings, insufficient single-pump head.

**Expand "Parallel Pump Modeling" section** (~500 words): Currently good but add: (1) Flow distribution depends on individual pump curves — unequal curves cause unequal flow split, (2) Each pump sees same head but different flow, (3) NPSH margin must be checked for EACH pump separately, (4) Duty/standby configuration (one runs, one on backup) vs simultaneous operation.

**Expand "Series Pump Modeling" section** (~500 words): Add: (1) Combined head is sum of individual heads at given flow rate, (2) Each pump sees same flow but different suction/discharge pressures, (3) NPSH critical — first pump must not cavitate or second pump sees vapor, (4) Interstage pressure must be reasonable (not excessive).

**Add "Common Mistakes" section** (~400 words): (1) Parallel pumps with mismatched curves (flow imbalance, one pump starves), (2) Series pumps without NPSH check on first pump (cavitation), (3) Duty/standby modeled as simultaneous operation (oversizing), (4) Ignoring check valve resistance in parallel configuration.

**Add "FluidFlow Implementation" subsection** (~300 words): Explain how to build parallel/series models on flowsheet — parallel pumps share common headers, series pumps connected in sequence. FluidFlow automatically combines curves and distributes flow/head.
**Add "Validation Checklist"**: Verify total flow matches sum of parallel pumps, total head matches sum of series pumps, each pump duty point within curve range, NPSH adequate for all pumps.
**Add "Real-World Applications" examples**: Cooling tower systems (parallel pumps for redundancy), high-rise buildings (series booster stations), fire protection (duty/standby jockey pumps).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper parallel/series modeling to avoiding pump cavitation and ensuring reliable system operation.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).

2 — "System-level analysis, not isolated component checks" — weave into opening: FluidFlow calculates flow distribution among parallel pumps and interstage pressures in series pumps automatically, ensuring proper operation across all pump combinations, not just nameplate ratings.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Parallel pumps: Combined curve (horizontal addition of flow at constant head), flow distribution calculation
Series pumps: Combined curve (vertical addition of head at constant flow), interstage pressure calculation

NPSH calculation: Individual calculation for each pump in configuration
Check valves: Pressure drop through check valves in parallel configuration

Head calculation: Series pumps add head incrementally

MINI PRD: P26 — Multi-Component Fluid Mixtures
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P26 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 1800-2500
What This Article Does Now

Guide to modeling multi-component fluid mixtures in FluidFlow, particularly for two-phase systems where multiple pure components (water, hydrocarbons, refrigerants) are mixed. Explains how FluidFlow uses standard mixing rules to calculate mixture physical properties (density, enthalpy, vapor pressure) from component properties. Covers mole fraction vs mass fraction input, flash calculations for phase equilibrium, and how mixture properties affect pressure drop and heat transfer calculations. Includes refrigerant blends and hydrocarbon mixtures.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Strengthen engineering context explaining WHEN mixture modeling is required (refrigerant systems, hydrocarbon processing, chemical plants), HOW FluidFlow handles phase equilibrium (flash calculations at each node), and WHY standard mixing rules are appropriate for most engineering applications. Add validation guidance — when to trust mixing rules vs when rigorous process simulation is needed. Include practical examples: R-410A refrigerant (R-32 + R-125 blend), natural gas mixtures, process condensates. Strengthen connection to real-world two-phase applications.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that industrial piping systems often transport mixtures (refrigerant blends, natural gas, process condensates) where physical properties depend on composition — FluidFlow calculates mixture properties using standard mixing rules and performs flash calculations at each node, ensuring accurate two-phase flow predictions.

**Add "When Mixture Modeling is Required" section** (~300 words): Single-component fluids (pure water, pure propane) use simple property correlations. Multi-component fluids (refrigerant blends, natural gas, hydrocarbon mixtures, chemical process streams) require mixing rules to determine density, enthalpy, vapor pressure, and phase distribution.

**Add "Mixing Rule Background" section** (~350 words): Explain that FluidFlow uses standard mixing rules (Kay's Rule for pseudo-critical properties, mole-weighted mixing for some properties). These are industry-standard methods appropriate for most engineering applications. When rigorous process simulation is needed (highly non-ideal mixtures, chemical reactions), specialized software may be required.

**Expand "Flash Calculations" section** (~400 words): Explain that two-phase systems continuously change composition as light components vaporize and heavy components condense. FluidFlow performs flash calculations (vapor-liquid equilibrium) at each node using selected EOS (Peng-Robinson preferred for hydrocarbons/refrigerants), updating mixture properties as phase fractions change along pipe length.

**Add "Component Input Methods"** (~250 words): Mole fraction (chemists/process engineers typically use this) vs mass fraction (field engineers often prefer this). FluidFlow accepts both, converts internally for calculations.

**Add "Practical Examples" (~400 words): (1) R-410A refrigerant: 50/50 blend of R-32 + R-125, used in HVAC. (2) Natural gas: Methane + ethane + propane + butane + CO₂ + N₂, composition varies by source. (3) Process condensate: Multi-component hydrocarbon liquid from separator, properties change with pressure/temperature.
**Add "EOS Selection for Mixtures"**: Peng-Robinson EOS is generally most accurate for hydrocarbon and refrigerant mixtures. Lee-Kesler for simpler systems. BWRHS for broad property ranges.
**Add "Validation Approach"**: Compare calculated mixture properties (density, vapor pressure) to lab data or literature values at known conditions to validate mixing rule accuracy.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper mixture modeling to accurate two-phase pressure drop and avoiding undersized relief systems.

MINI PRD: P27 — Modeling Positive Displacement Pumps
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P27 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now

Guide to modeling positive displacement (PD) pumps in FluidFlow, covering piston, diaphragm, gear, screw, and lobe pump types. Explains how PD pumps deliver nearly constant flow regardless of head (within pressure limits), contrasting with centrifugal pumps where flow varies with system curve. Documents volumetric efficiency modeling, slip losses, and maximum discharge pressure limits. Includes practical examples showing PD pump applications (high-viscosity fluids, precise metering, high-pressure systems).
What It Should Do After Refactor

Strengthen engineering context explaining WHEN PD pumps are preferred over centrifugal (viscous fluids >100 cSt, metering applications, self-priming requirements, high pressure >10 bar), HOW FluidFlow models slip and efficiency losses, and WHY pressure relief protection is mandatory for PD pumps (dead-heading risk). Add decision framework for PD pump type selection. Include validation guidance for volumetric efficiency and maximum pressure rating. Strengthen connection to real-world applications (chemical dosing, hydraulic systems, high-viscosity transfer).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that positive displacement pumps deliver constant flow independent of system resistance (within pressure limits) — FluidFlow models PD pumps with volumetric efficiency corrections and pressure limit enforcement, ensuring accurate performance prediction for metering, high-viscosity, and high-pressure applications.

**Add "PD vs Centrifugal Decision Matrix"** (~400 words): When to use PD pumps — high viscosity (>100 cSt, centrifugal efficiency drops dramatically), precise metering (dosing pumps, chemical injection), self-priming (suction lift, vapor handling), high pressure (>10 bar discharge, centrifugal multi-staging not practical), shear-sensitive fluids (food, polymers). When centrifugal is better — low viscosity, variable flow, lower initial cost.

**Expand "PD Pump Types" section** (~400 words): (1) Reciprocating (piston, plunger, diaphragm): High pressure capability, pulsating flow, lower speed. (2) Rotary (gear, screw, lobe, vane): Smooth flow, moderate pressure, higher speed. Selection based on pressure, viscosity, flow rate, and fluid compatibility.

**Expand "Volumetric Efficiency" section** (~350 words): PD pumps don't deliver theoretical displacement due to slip (internal leakage), compression effects, and fill/discharge valve losses. Efficiency typically 85-98% depending on pump type, speed, viscosity, and pressure rise. FluidFlow models efficiency vs pressure rise for accurate flow prediction.

**Add "Pressure Relief Requirements"** (~300 words): CRITICAL SAFETY POINT — PD pumps can generate unlimited pressure if discharge is blocked (dead-heading). Relief valves or rupture discs MANDATORY on discharge side. FluidFlow warns if calculated discharge pressure exceeds pump maximum rating.

**Add "Slip and Leakage Modeling"**: Internal slip increases with pressure differential and decreases with viscosity (thick fluids seal clearances better). FluidFlow accounts for slip-based efficiency reduction.

**Add "Applications Examples"** (~300 words): Chemical dosing (diaphragm pumps, precise flow), hydraulic systems (piston pumps, high pressure), food processing (lobe pumps, sanitary design, shear-sensitive), crude oil transfer (screw pumps, high viscosity).

**Add "Validation Checklist"**: Verify discharge pressure within pump rating, efficiency appropriate for viscosity and pressure, relief valve sized adequately, NPSH adequate (PD pumps still require NPSHa > NPSHr).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper PD pump modeling to avoiding safety hazards and accurate metering performance.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).

MINI PRD: P29 — Viscosity Correction for Centrifugal Pumps
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P29 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 1800-2500
What This Article Does Now

Technical guide on how viscosity affects centrifugal pump performance and how FluidFlow applies correction factors. Explains that centrifugal pumps are typically rated on water (1 cP viscosity) but performance degrades with higher-viscosity fluids due to increased internal friction and reduced turbulence. Documents viscosity correction methods available in FluidFlow for adjusting head, flow, and efficiency. Includes guidance on when corrections are necessary (typically above 20 cSt viscosity).
What It Should Do After Refactor

Strengthen engineering context explaining WHY viscosity corrections matter (ignoring corrections can result in 30-50% flow reduction for oils, syrups, polymers), WHEN corrections are required (typically >20 cSt, mandatory >100 cSt), and HOW FluidFlow implements industry-standard correction methods. Add viscosity threshold guidance and practical examples showing performance degradation. Include validation approach (compare corrected curves to manufacturer viscous-fluid test data). Strengthen connection to avoiding pump under-performance failures.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that centrifugal pump curves are published for water (1 cP viscosity) but performance degrades significantly for viscous fluids (oils, syrups, polymers) — FluidFlow applies industry-standard viscosity corrections ensuring accurate duty point prediction and avoiding undersized pump selections.

**Add "Viscosity Effects on Pump Performance" section** (~400 words): Explain physical mechanisms — viscous fluids increase internal recirculation losses, reduce turbulence (lower efficiency), increase friction in impeller passages (lower head). Performance degradation is negligible below 20 cSt, moderate 20-100 cSt (5-15% head reduction), severe above 100 cSt (can exceed 50% flow reduction). FluidFlow warns when viscosity corrections should be applied.

**Add "When Corrections Are Required" table**: <20 cSt (water-like, corrections optional), 20-100 cSt (moderate viscosity, corrections recommended), >100 cSt (high viscosity, corrections mandatory), >1000 cSt (very high, centrifugal pumps may not be suitable, consider PD pumps per P27).

**Expand "Correction Methods" section** (~400 words): FluidFlow implements standard viscosity correction procedures (likely Hydraulic Institute standards or manufacturer methods — verify against spec sheet). Corrections adjust head-flow curve, efficiency curve, and power curve. Corrected curves show reduced flow at same head, lower efficiency at all points, shifted BEP location.

**Add "Practical Examples" (~400 words): (1) Lube oil transfer (SAE 30 oil, ~100 cSt at 40°C): Water curve shows 100 m³/h at 30m head, viscosity correction reduces to ~85 m³/h at 30m head, efficiency drops from 75% to 65%. (2) Hydraulic fluid (ISO VG 68, ~68 cSt at 40°C): Moderate corrections apply. (3) Heavy syrup (500 cSt): Severe corrections, centrifugal pump may not be viable.

**Add "Temperature Dependence"**: Viscosity strongly temperature-dependent (oil viscosity can vary 10× between 20°C and 80°C). FluidFlow accounts for temperature effects on viscosity and recalculates corrections at actual operating temperature.

**Add "Validation Approach"** (~250 words): If manufacturer provides viscous-fluid test curves, compare FluidFlow corrected curve to manufacturer data. Typical agreement within ±5% for validated correction methods.
**Add "When to Consider PD Pumps"**: Above ~1000 cSt, centrifugal efficiency becomes very poor. Positive displacement pumps (P27) may be more suitable for very high viscosity applications.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper viscosity correction to avoiding pump under-performance and system flow shortfalls.
